class_schedule.topic: The Cognitive Revolution and Information Processing
learning_objectives: Trace the historical emergence of cognitive psychology from behaviorism
Describe the information-processing framework and its computer metaphor
Identify modern methods for studying cognition: reaction time, eye-tracking, neuroimaging

lecture_notes.introduction: Cognitive psychology studies the internal mental processes that underlie perception, attention, memory, language, and decision-making. The cognitive revolution of the 1950s-60s replaced strict behaviorism with a focus on mental representations.
key_concepts: Chomsky's critique of Skinner: language is too complex for simple reinforcement
Information-processing model: input, encoding, storage, retrieval, output
Levels of analysis: computational, algorithmic, implementational (Marr)
Methods: chronometry, priming, dual-task paradigms, fMRI, EEG
Ecological validity vs. experimental control in cognitive research
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mental rotation task: measure reaction times as rotation angle increases (Shepard & Metzler replication)

review.value: The mind processes information in stages analogous to a computer: input, processing, storage, and output
Computational (what problem is solved), algorithmic (how it is solved), implementational (neural hardware)

learning_objectives: Compare early-selection and late-selection models of attention
Explain the cocktail party effect and inattentional blindness
Describe the role of attention in everyday tasks such as driving

lecture_notes.introduction: Attention determines what information enters conscious processing. Without selective attention, we would be overwhelmed by the flood of sensory input.
key_concepts: Broadbent's filter model (early selection)
Deutsch & Deutsch's late-selection model
Treisman's attenuation model
Divided attention and dual-task performance
Inattentional blindness and change blindness
lecture_notes.demonstration: Selective attention demonstration: count basketball passes while a gorilla walks through (Simons & Chabris)

review.value: Failure to notice a fully visible but unexpected stimulus when attention is engaged elsewhere
Unattended information is weakened (attenuated) but not fully blocked; meaningful stimuli can break through

learning_objectives: Describe the multi-store model and working memory model
Explain encoding specificity and transfer-appropriate processing
Distinguish between explicit and implicit memory systems

lecture_notes.introduction: Memory is not a single system but a collection of interacting processes and stores. Understanding how encoding, storage, and retrieval work reveals why we remember some things and forget others.
key_concepts: Atkinson-Shiffrin model: sensory, short-term, long-term memory
Baddeley's working memory: phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, central executive, episodic buffer
Levels of processing (Craik & Lockhart): shallow vs. deep encoding
Encoding specificity and state-dependent memory
Explicit (declarative) vs. implicit (procedural) memory
lecture_notes.demonstration: Levels of processing experiment: compare recall for words encoded structurally, phonemically, and semantically
setup_requirements: Word lists for encoding depth experiment

review.value: Baddeley's model: a limited-capacity system for temporary storage and manipulation of information during complex tasks
Deeper semantic processing leads to stronger memory traces than shallow structural or phonemic processing

learning_objectives: Describe the stages of language comprehension: phonemes, morphemes, syntax, semantics
Explain models of sentence parsing and garden-path sentences
Discuss the relationship between language and thought (Sapir-Whorf hypothesis)

lecture_notes.introduction: Language is the most complex cognitive ability humans possess. Understanding how we produce and comprehend language reveals fundamental principles of cognitive architecture.
key_concepts: Hierarchical structure of language: phonemes, morphemes, syntax, semantics, pragmatics
Garden-path sentences and syntactic ambiguity resolution
Spreading activation in semantic memory
Speech production: from conceptualization to articulation (Levelt's model)
Linguistic relativity: weak vs. strong Sapir-Whorf hypothesis
lecture_notes.demonstration: Garden-path sentence exercise: students parse ambiguous sentences and identify the reanalysis point

review.value: Sentences where the reader is initially led down the wrong syntactic interpretation and must reparse: 'The horse raced past the barn fell'
Activating one concept in semantic memory spreads activation to related concepts, facilitating their retrieval

learning_objectives: Describe algorithms, heuristics, and insight in problem solving
Explain major cognitive biases: availability, representativeness, anchoring
Compare normative and descriptive models of decision making

lecture_notes.introduction: Humans are not perfectly rational decision-makers. Cognitive psychology has revealed systematic biases and heuristics that shape our judgments and choices.
key_concepts: Well-defined vs. ill-defined problems
Functional fixedness and mental set
Tversky and Kahneman's heuristics and biases program
Prospect theory: loss aversion and framing effects
Dual-process theory: System 1 (fast, intuitive) vs. System 2 (slow, deliberate)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Bias demonstration: present framing effect scenario (surgery survival rates) and anchoring task (estimate UN country count)

review.value: Judging probability by how easily examples come to mind; leads to overestimating vivid, recent, or memorable events
People weigh losses more heavily than equivalent gains; risk-averse for gains, risk-seeking for losses

learning_objectives: Explain how neuroimaging has advanced cognitive theory
Describe neural correlates of attention, memory, and executive function
Evaluate the promise and limitations of computational models of cognition

lecture_notes.introduction: Modern cognitive psychology increasingly integrates with neuroscience, using brain imaging to constrain and refine cognitive theories.
key_concepts: fMRI, EEG, and TMS: methods for linking brain and cognition
Prefrontal cortex and executive function
Hippocampus and memory consolidation
Neural networks and connectionist models
Embodied cognition and challenges to the computer metaphor
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analysis of fMRI data from a published working memory study: identify activated regions and relate to cognitive theory

review.value: If damage to area A impairs function X but not Y, and damage to area B impairs Y but not X, the two functions are neurally independent
Cognition is not abstract symbol manipulation but is grounded in bodily experience and sensorimotor interaction with the world

questions.question: Explain the difference between Broadbent's early-selection model and Treisman's attenuation model. What experimental finding prompted the revision?
questions.answer: Broadbent proposed that unattended information is completely filtered out at an early stage based on physical characteristics. Treisman revised this to an attenuation model where unattended information is weakened but not eliminated. The revision was prompted by the cocktail party effect: people can detect their own name in an unattended channel, which is impossible if that channel is completely blocked as Broadbent proposed.

rubric.key: Identifies cocktail party effect as motivating evidence

questions.question: Describe Baddeley's working memory model, naming all four components and explaining the function of each.
questions.answer: The phonological loop stores and rehearses verbal/acoustic information (inner voice and inner ear). The visuospatial sketchpad handles visual and spatial information. The central executive is an attention-like control system that coordinates the subsystems, switches attention, and manages cognitive resources. The episodic buffer integrates information from the subsystems and long-term memory into coherent episodes.

rubric.key: Notes the coordinating role of central executive

quizzes.topic: Language, Problem Solving, and Decision Making

questions.question: What is a garden-path sentence? Provide an example and explain the cognitive process of reanalysis that occurs when reading one.
questions.answer: A garden-path sentence is syntactically ambiguous in a way that leads the reader to initially adopt an incorrect parse. Example: 'The old man the boats.' The reader initially parses 'old' as an adjective and 'man' as a noun, but the correct parse treats 'old' as a noun (the old people) and 'man' as a verb (operate). The reader hits a dead end at 'the boats' and must reanalyze the sentence structure, engaging controlled processing resources.

questions.question: A doctor tells Patient A there is a 90% survival rate for a surgery, and tells Patient B there is a 10% mortality rate. Both patients have the same surgery. Using prospect theory, explain why Patient B might be more reluctant to proceed.
questions.answer: This is a framing effect explained by prospect theory. Although 90% survival and 10% mortality are logically identical, prospect theory shows that people are loss-averse: losses (mortality) loom larger than equivalent gains (survival). The mortality frame makes the risk salient and triggers risk-averse behavior, while the survival frame emphasizes the positive outcome. System 1 (fast, intuitive) processing responds to the frame rather than computing the equivalence.

rubric.key: Identifies this as a framing effect
Explains loss aversion from prospect theory
Notes the logical equivalence of the two frames

major_assignments.title: Cognitive Bias Field Study
major_assignments.description: Design and conduct a small-scale replication of a classic cognitive psychology experiment (e.g., Stroop effect, levels of processing, framing effect). Collect data from at least 20 participants, analyze results, and write a full APA-format research report.
objectives: Replicate a published cognitive experiment with methodological rigor
Collect and analyze behavioral data using appropriate statistics
Interpret results in the context of cognitive theory
deliverables: Raw data file and analysis output
Stimulus materials used in the experiment

criteria: Appropriate sample size and participant recruitment
Effect size and confidence intervals included
Results discussed in relation to cognitive theory
Rigorous replication with appropriate methodology and controls
Accurate statistical analysis with effect sizes and confidence intervals
Insightful discussion connecting results to cognitive theory

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of cognitive psychology concepts and theories

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on cognitive experiments and data analysis exercises

major_assignments.description: Cognitive experiment replication study with full APA report

midterm_examination.description: Exam covering attention, perception, and memory

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all cognitive topics

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is expected. Lab sessions involving data collection require attendance for the experimental protocol to function.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Data collection deadlines are firm due to participant scheduling constraints.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab partners may collect data together, but each student writes their own independent report. Sharing written analysis is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Data fabrication or falsification will result in course failure. All analyses must use genuinely collected data.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to experiment software (PsychoPy or E-Prime), SPSS or JASP, and APA writing resources.
